g.data-narration: Welcome to Euler's Theorem! Today we'll explore how homogeneous functions of any degree relate beautifully to their partial derivatives in calculus and applied mathematics.
A function is homogeneous of degree n if replacing x and y with t times x and y gives t to the power n times the original function.
g.text: f(tx, ty) = tⁿ f(x, y) for all t > 0
Example: f(x,y) = x² + y² → f(tx,ty) = t²(x² + y²) = t²f(x,y) → degree n = 2

g.data-narration: Euler's Theorem states that for any homogeneous function of degree n, the sum of variables times their partial derivatives equals n times the function.
g.text: Or using subscript notation: x·fₓ + y·fᵧ = n·f

g.data-narration: For three variables, Euler's Theorem extends by adding a third term: x times the partial derivative with respect to x, plus y and z terms, equals n times f.
g.text: General form for n variables: Σᵢ xᵢ(∂f/∂xᵢ) = n · f

g.data-narration: Let's verify Euler's Theorem using f(x,y) equals x to the fourth power plus two x cubed y plus x y cubed. First, we'll check if it's homogeneous.
g.text: Step 1: Check homogeneity → f(tx,ty) = t⁴(x⁴ + 2x³y + xy³) = t⁴f(x,y)

g.data-narration: Now we compute the partial derivatives: the partial with respect to x gives four x cubed plus six x squared y plus y cubed. The partial with respect to y gives two x cubed plus three x y squared.
Applying Euler's theorem: x times the x-partial plus y times the y-partial simplifies to four times f of x y, confirming the theorem. Key point: always multiply by n!
g.text: Key: Don't forget the degree n! Common mistake: writing =f instead of =n·f
Tip: Always verify homogeneity first before applying Euler's Theorem
